title: Cat Dried Blood on Penis Tip: Causes and When to See a Vet
meta_desc: Noticed dried blood on your cat's penis? Learn about urinary issues, why it is a medium-urgency concern, and how photos help your vet triage.
content_html:
What Could This Mean?

Finding dried blood on the tip of your cat's penis is often a sign of irritation or inflammation within the lower urinary tract. This condition, often referred to as Feline Lower Urinary Tract Disease (FLUTD), can be caused by various factors including urinary tract infections (UTIs), sterile cystitis, or the presence of crystals or stones in the bladder. In some cases, the blood may be the result of over-grooming due to discomfort or minor trauma to the area.

Is It an Emergency?

The urgency level for dried blood on the penis is generally considered Medium. While the presence of blood alone requires a veterinary appointment within 24 hours, the situation becomes a critical, life-threatening emergency if your male cat is straining to urinate, vocalizing in the litter box, or unable to pass any urine at all. Urinary blockages are common in male cats and can become fatal quickly if not treated immediately. If your cat is still eating, drinking, and urinating normally, you should still schedule a prompt visit to rule out underlying infections or stones.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Providing your veterinarian with a clear photo of the area can be incredibly helpful for remote triage. A photo allows the medical team to see the exact color and amount of the discharge, the condition of the surrounding tissue, and whether there is any swelling or active bleeding. This visual information helps them determine how quickly your cat needs to be seen and prepares them for the specific diagnostic tests, such as a urinalysis or imaging, that your cat may require upon arrival.

faqs.question: Can I wait a few days to see if the blood goes away?
faqs.answer: It is not recommended to wait. Urinary issues in male cats can progress rapidly into life-threatening blockages. It is best to have a vet examine your cat within 24 hours.

faqs.question: What are the signs of a urinary blockage?
faqs.answer: Watch for frequent trips to the litter box with little to no urine produced, crying out while trying to pee, excessive licking of the genital area, and lethargy.

faqs.question: Will my cat need tests to find the cause?
faqs.answer: Yes, your veterinarian will likely recommend a urinalysis to check for infection or crystals, and potentially an X-ray or ultrasound to look for bladder stones.
